metadata.title: B1 Preliminary Gold Mock 3
metadata.description: M4.1 academic reference mock for B1 Preliminary — Career, Relationships, and Global topics across reading, writing, listening, and speaking.
metadata.authoringNotes: M4.1 Gold Mock — manually authored and academically reviewed.

parts.title: Part 1 — Listen and choose the correct picture
parts.instructions: Part 1 — Listen and choose the correct picture

audio.transcript: Woman: I'm asking about internship forms at the careers desk. Friends: We're celebrating a reunion with food in the park.
audio.caption: Part 1 — Listen and choose the correct picture

parts.title: Part 2 — Listen and choose the best answer
parts.instructions: Part 2 — Listen and choose the best answer

audio.transcript: Girl: I missed mentoring because of a family appointment. Boy: After school I'm going to a university open day.
audio.caption: Part 2 — Listen and choose the best answer

parts.title: Part 3 — Listen and complete each gap
parts.instructions: Part 3 — Listen and complete each gap

audio.transcript: Organiser: The careers webinar starts at 5 p.m. Registration costs ten pounds. The mentor will reply by Friday.
audio.caption: Part 3 — Listen and complete each gap

audio.transcript: Speaker: I met my mentor at a school careers event. I schedule calls with friends abroad and practise interviews twice a week.
audio.caption: Part 4 — Listen to the monologue

parts.title: Part 5 — Listen and match each speaker to an activity
parts.instructions: Part 5 — Listen and match each speaker to an activity

audio.transcript: Five students describe hospital work experience, long-distance friendship, Model UN, coding internship, and peer mediation.
audio.caption: Part 5 — Listen and match each speaker to an activity

questions.questionText: Careers fair — meet employers from twelve countries, hall open until 4 p.m.

choices.text: Students can talk to international employers this afternoon.
Only teachers may enter the hall.

questions.questionText: Language exchange tonight — practise English and Spanish with visiting students.

choices.text: People can practise languages with visitors tonight.
The exchange is for staff only.

questions.questionText: Peer support room — talk confidentially about friendship or family worries.

choices.text: Students can discuss personal worries in private.
The room is for exam revision only.

questions.questionText: Internship applications close Monday — CV workshop Friday at 3 p.m.

choices.text: Students can get CV help before the deadline.
The workshop is on Monday morning.

questions.questionText: World cultures day — traditional food and music from five continents.

choices.text: Visitors can experience food and music from many regions.
Only local food will be served.

questions.questionText: Conflict resolution session — learn strategies for healthy discussions.

choices.text: Participants will learn how to discuss disagreements calmly.

questions.questionText: Who is doing a marketing internship?

pairs.rightText: is working for an international charity

questions.questionText: Who counsels students about friendship problems?
Who hosts a podcast about working abroad?
Who has organised a multicultural food festival?

pairs.rightText: has raised money for exchange trips

questions.questionText: Who is going to apply for a nursing course?

pairs.rightText: plans to study health care at college

questions.questionText: Why was Amira worried at first?

content.passage: When Amira moved abroad for a summer internship at an international charity, she worried about communicating in English every day. Her mentor paired her with a local volunteer who showed her how to organise online meetings across time zones. Amira learned to write clear emails to partners in three countries and to listen carefully during video calls. By the end of the programme, she had helped launch a campaign about clean water in rural schools. The experience strengthened her confidence and showed her how global teamwork can improve communities far from home.

choices.text: She had to use English every day abroad
She did not like charity work

questions.questionText: Gap (1): future work seem less ___

content.passage: Choosing a career path can feel stressful when friends already talk about university plans. Career advisers suggest exploring internships, part-time jobs, and volunteering to discover what skills you enjoy. Strong relationships with teachers and family often help teenagers handle pressure and make realistic goals. Global companies increasingly value employees who speak more than one language and understand different cultures. Some schools invite alumni to share stories about their first jobs, which makes future work seem less (1) ___. Mentors recommend practising interviews with a friend so answers sound natural rather than (2) ___. When students disagree with parents about career choices, calm conversations usually work better than (3) ___ arguments. International exchange programmes build friendships that last for years and open doors to (4) ___ opportunities. Employers also notice candidates who show empathy and communicate clearly in (5) ___ teams. In a connected world, combining personal relationships with global awareness can lead to a more (6) ___ career journey.

questions.questionText: Which sentence introduces friendships across borders?

content.passage: [A] Many teenagers stay in touch with friends who move to other countries. [B] Messaging apps make it easy to share news across continents within seconds. [C] However, long-distance friendships need trust and regular communication. [D] Some students schedule weekly video calls so they do not lose contact. [E] Cultural differences can cause misunderstandings, but they also teach patience. [F] Maintaining global relationships helps young people feel connected and more open-minded.

choices.text: [A] Many teenagers stay in touch with friends who move to other countries.
[B] Messaging apps make it easy to share news across continents within seconds.
[F] Maintaining global relationships helps young people feel connected and more open-minded.

questions.questionText: Write an email in response to your English teacher about career.

content.prompt: Your English teacher has asked you to write about your future career plans. Write an email to your teacher.
content.taskDescription: Write about 100 words. Answer all three points.
requiredPoints: Describe a job or field you are interested in
Explain what skills or experience you need
Ask for advice about steps you could take now

questions.questionText: Write a story about relationships that begins with the sentence below.

content.prompt: Your teacher has asked you to write a story. Your story must begin with this sentence: 'I had not seen my best friend for two years, but the message arrived at the perfect moment.'

questions.questionText: Which speaker talks about a hospital work experience?

questions.questionText: Which speaker describes a Model United Nations club?
Which speaker joined a coding internship?
Which speaker volunteers as a peer mediator?
Answer the examiner's questions about career.

content.prompt: The examiner will ask about your school subjects, career interests, and plans for the future.
followUpQuestions: Which school subjects do you enjoy most? Why?
Have you ever done work experience or volunteering?
What kind of job would you like in the future?
Do you prefer working alone or in a team?
What skills do you think are important for your chosen career?

questions.questionText: Describe the global and say what you think is happening.

content.prompt: Look at the photograph. Describe the setting, the people, and what you think they are doing.

questions.questionText: Discuss how young people can maintain strong relationships in a global world.

content.prompt: Talk with your partner about keeping friendships and family relationships healthy when people live far apart.
followUpQuestions: What are the best ways to stay in touch with friends abroad?
How can misunderstandings be avoided in online conversations?
Should schools teach communication skills formally?
What role do parents play in supporting friendships?
How does social media help or harm relationships?
Which habit has worked best for you or someone you know?
